sentences.grammarNote: 好久不见 is a fixed phrase literally meaning 'a long time not seen.' It's the standard 'long time no see' — Chinese grammar here has famously influenced English.
呢 (ne) at the end of a short phrase asks 'what about…?' or 'and you?'. It bounces the same question back to the other person without repeating the whole question.
Question words in Chinese stay in the same position as the answer would. 哪儿 (where) sits where the destination would be: 你去哪儿 vs 我去超市 (I'm going to the supermarket). Never move them to the front.

sentences.base_language: I'm going to the coffee shop. Do you want to come?
sentences.romanized: Wǒ qù kāfēidiàn. Nǐ xiǎng lái ma?
sentences.literal: I go coffee-shop. You want-to come?

sentences.base_language: Sure! I know a good one nearby.
sentences.target_language: 好啊！我知道附近有一家不错的。
sentences.romanized: Hǎo a! Wǒ zhīdào fùjìn yǒu yī jiā bùcuò de.
sentences.literal: Good! I know nearby has one not-bad one.
sentences.grammarNote: 家 (jiā) is the measure word for shops, businesses, and restaurants: 一家咖啡店, 一家医院. 知道 (zhīdào) = 'to know a fact'; 认识 (rènshi) = 'to know a person'. These are distinct in Chinese.

sentences.romanized: Tài hǎo le, wǒmen yīqǐ qù ba.
sentences.grammarNote: 太…了 is an intensifier: 太好了 = 'great! / too good!'. 一起 (yīqǐ) = 'together', placed before the verb.

sentences.base_language: It was nice running into you.
